name: Cañón del río Izana y ermita de los Santos Mártires – circular desde Las Cuevas de Soria
region_intro: Hiking around Quintana Redonda offers a diverse landscape characterized by river canyons, forested areas, and elevated viewpoints. The region features a mix of moderate and more challenging trails, often leading through natural features like ancient trees and along watercourses. These routes provide opportunities to explore the varied terrain of Quintana Redonda, from river valleys to scenic overlooks. Best hiking trails around Quintana Redonda The most popular hiking route is Cañón del río Izana y ermita de los Santos Mártires – circular desde Las Cuevas de Soria, a 6.9 miles (11.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 3 minutes to complete. This route explores the Izana river canyon and leads to a hermitage. Another top favourite among local hikers is Mirador Portillo de Andaluz y Refugio Palomar Risco – circular desde Andaluz, a moderate 7.8 miles (12.6 km) path. This trail offers scenic viewpoints and passes by a refuge. Local hikers also love the Forest Shelter of La Fuentona – La Fuentona de Muriel loop from Calatañazor, a 13.5 miles (21.8 km) trail leading through forested areas, often completed in about 5 hours 49 minutes. Hiking around Quintana Redonda is defined by river canyons, forested landscapes, and elevated viewpoints. The network offers options for different ability levels, including moderate and difficult trails. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 4 hikers have used komoot to explore Quintana Redonda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Quintana Redonda?
A: There are &lt;strong&gt;5&lt;/strong&gt; distinct hiking routes documented around Quintana Redonda on komoot. These trails offer a variety of experiences, from moderate walks to more challenging excursions through the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly hiking trails in Quintana Redonda?
A: While the guide primarily features moderate to difficult trails, the region's terrain, characterized by river canyons and forested areas, means that even moderate routes can be enjoyed by those with some hiking experience. For a moderate option that explores natural features, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1062425429/canon-del-rio-izana-y-ermita-de-los-santos-martires-circular-desde-las-cuevas-de-soria" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañón del río Izana y ermita de los Santos Mártires – circular desde Las Cuevas de Soria&lt;/a&gt;, which is 6.9 miles (11.1 km) long.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Quintana Redonda?
A: Many of the moderate trails around Quintana Redonda can be suitable for families with older children or those accustomed to walking. The region's natural beauty, including river canyons and ancient trees, provides engaging scenery. For a route that passes by ancient trees, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40384380" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ancient Tree loop from Rioseco de Soria&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 5.6-mile (9 km) option.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Quintana Redonda?
A: Quintana Redonda's natural trails are generally welcoming to dogs, provided they are kept under control and local regulations are respected. The varied terrain, from riverbanks to forests, offers plenty for pets to enjoy alongside their owners. Always ensure to clean up after your dog.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes available in the area?
A: Yes, several circular routes are available, allowing you to start and end your hike in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063946620/mirador-portillo-de-andaluz-y-refugio-palomar-risco-circular-desde-andaluz" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador Portillo de Andaluz y Refugio Palomar Risco – circular desde Andaluz&lt;/a&gt;, which offers scenic viewpoints, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24549810" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Pozos – Las Fuentes loop from Villaciervos&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trails around Quintana Redonda?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. Many routes, especially those starting from villages like Andaluz or Las Cuevas de Soria, typically have designated parking areas or roadside parking options nearby. It's advisable to check the specific route details on komoot for more precise parking information before you go.

Q: What kind of scenic viewpoints can I expect on hikes in Quintana Redonda?
A: The region is known for its elevated viewpoints, offering expansive vistas of the surrounding landscape. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063946620/mirador-portillo-de-andaluz-y-refugio-palomar-risco-circular-desde-andaluz" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador Portillo de Andaluz y Refugio Palomar Risco – circular desde Andaluz&lt;/a&gt; route specifically highlights a scenic viewpoint. You can also visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5587359" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Andaluz Geodetic Vertex (1,027 m)&lt;/a&gt; for panoramic views.

Q: What interesting landmarks or attractions can I see along the hiking trails?
A: Hiking around Quintana Redonda offers opportunities to discover various points of interest. You might encounter historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7214625" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Necropolis&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5310201" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Holy Martyrs&lt;/a&gt;. Natural features include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3942164" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinar de Camparañón&lt;/a&gt; forest or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5584231" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna del Ciego&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1062425429/canon-del-rio-izana-y-ermita-de-los-santos-martires-circular-desde-las-cuevas-de-soria" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañón del río Izana y ermita de los Santos Mártires – circular desde Las Cuevas de Soria&lt;/a&gt; route, for example, leads to a hermitage.

Q: What is the best season for hiking in Quintana Redonda?
A: The diverse landscape of Quintana Redonda, with its river canyons and forests, makes it enjoyable across multiple seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, ideal for exploring. Summer can be warm, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, while winter might offer unique, quieter experiences, especially in forested areas like those found on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17543398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Forest Shelter of La Fuentona – La Fuentona de Muriel loop from Calatañazor&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any less crowded hiking options for a peaceful experience?
A: While specific crowd levels can vary, opting for longer or more challenging routes, or hiking during off-peak hours (early mornings, weekdays), can often lead to a more tranquil experience. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17543398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Forest Shelter of La Fuentona – La Fuentona de Muriel loop from Calatañazor&lt;/a&gt;, being a difficult 13.5-mile (21.8 km) trail, might offer more solitude due to its length.

Q: Are there any hikes that pass by cafes or places to eat?
A: While the hiking routes themselves often traverse natural and rural areas, many trails start or end in villages where you can find local cafes or restaurants. It's recommended to check the starting and ending points of your chosen route, such as those beginning in Andaluz or Rioseco de Soria, for nearby amenities.
tips: Beautiful enclave from which we can observe the canyon formed by the Izana river. Access on foot from Cuevas de Soria will not require any physical effort and the path is in good condition.

The Hermitage of the Holy Martyrs is located in the town of Las Cuevas de Soria, part of the municipality of Quintana Redonda, in the province of Soria (Castilla y León). Although not as well-known as other Romanesque hermitages in the region, it is part of the local religious and cultural heritage.
